---
title: "Architecture"
---

cositos exists to answer one question from its founding conversation: *how much work is it
to reuse an [anywidget](https://anywidget.dev) frontend from a kernel that isn't Python?*
The answer the design commits to: **the frontend and the protocol are shared; only a thin,
per-language adapter is not.** This page explains the shape that makes that true.

## The binding-free core

The central constraint — stated by anywidget's maintainer — is that the core must **not**
take on responsibility for per-language bindings. So cositos's core is **pure protocol
logic** with no kernel or transport code:

- **`protocol`** — constants, message builders (`build_comm_open`, `build_update`,
  `build_custom`), and the inbound parser (`parse_message`).
- **`buffers`** — the v2 nested binary-buffer split/merge rules.
- **`model`** — the `Widget` façade tying a state getter/setter to a transport.
- **`transport`** — the interface only. **No implementations live in the core.**

Because none of this touches I/O, the entire core is testable without a running kernel —
and portable to any language that can shape the same JSON.

## The Transport seam

Every kernel exposes comms differently: Python's `comm` library, Deno's
`Deno.jupyter.broadcast`, IJulia's comm API, dotnet-interactive. cositos absorbs that
variation behind a single narrow interface:

```
   host state object  ──▶  cositos core (pure, no I/O)
   (traits / struct)         open() -> comm_open payload
                             send_state() -> comm_msg payload
                             parse_message() -> typed inbound event
                             remove_buffers() / put_buffers()
                                     │
                                     │  Transport interface
                                     │    send(msg_type, content, buffers, metadata)
                                     │    on_message(callback)
                                     ▼
                          host adapter (per kernel, thin)
                            Python: comm.create_comm / on_msg
                            Deno:   Deno.jupyter.broadcast
                            Julia:  IJulia comm API
```

The seam is the only thing a new host must implement. Where a kernel is broadcast-only and
can't route frontend→kernel replies (early Deno), the adapter sets `supports_receive =
False` and the widget degrades gracefully to one-way.

## The Document is the virtual DOM

The unit of persistence and static rendering is the **Document**: a serialized,
JSON-safe snapshot of one or more widget models and their state (see
[serialization](../reference/specs.qmd#serialization)). It plays the role a virtual DOM
plays in a web framework — a **plain-data description of the UI**, decoupled from any
particular runtime:

- **Live kernel** replays a Document into `comm_open` + `update` messages over a
  `Transport`, and a real frontend renders it.
- **Static export** ([embed](../reference/specs.qmd#embed)) serializes the same Document
  into `widget-state+json` that the CDN html-manager renders with **no kernel at all**.

One data structure, two backends — the same reason a virtual DOM can render to the browser
or to a string. Composition falls out for free: a Document can hold a graph of widgets that
reference each other, and references survive the round-trip.

## The contract is data, not code

The cross-language guarantee is **not** a shared binary or FFI layer — it is a set of
**golden JSON fixtures** (`fixtures/`). A port in any language is correct if its message
builders and buffer split reproduce those fixtures byte-for-byte (modulo comm id and
ordering). This is what lets ecosystem experts own their language's bindings without the
core knowing anything about their language.

The [specifications](../reference/specs.qmd) formalize this: each requirement's scenarios
are the behaviour a fixture captures, and the [Julia port](../porting.md) certifies against
the very same `fixtures/widget-state.json` the Python reference does.

## Why reuse the anywidget frontend verbatim

cositos emits `_model_module = "anywidget"` and the `AnyModel`/`AnyView` identity, so the
**published anywidget frontend renders cositos widgets** with zero new JavaScript. That is
a deliberate trade: no frontend to maintain, and instant compatibility with the existing
widget ecosystem, at the cost of depending on anywidget's model/view contract remaining
stable.
